content.reason: No tier_topology yet — System Mapper must run to produce one before specialists can be selected.

narrative.what_this_demonstrates: A database with slow queries during business hours that cascade into elevated application latency on the compute tier. Compute itself is correctly sized; the problem is downstream.
narrative.why_this_scenario_exists: Tests cross-tier synthesis. The Compute Analyst will see latency elevated but compute capacity normal; the Data Layer Analyst will see slow queries; the Cross-Tier Evaluator must connect them and recommend query optimization rather than compute scaling.
narrative.what_the_agent_should_conclude: Optimize the top 6 slowest SQL queries and add 2 read replicas with read/write splitting. Expected outcome: application_p95_latency_ms drops from ~650ms business-hours to under 220ms.
narrative.what_distinguishes_good_from_great: A good recommendation identifies the database as the root cause. A great recommendation cites the specific top_queries fixture, pairs query optimization with replica scaling, and avoids recommending compute scaling (which would not fix the problem).

top_queries.query_text: SELECT c.*, ci.* FROM carts c JOIN cart_items ci ON ci.cart_id = c.id WHERE c.user_id = ?
SELECT i.*, p.* FROM inventory i JOIN products p ON p.id = i.product_id WHERE i.warehouse_id IN (...)
SELECT * FROM pricing WHERE product_id = ? AND region = ? AND valid_from <= NOW() AND valid_to > NOW() ORDER BY priority DESC
SELECT pp.* FROM payment_providers pp WHERE pp.country = ? AND pp.is_active = TRUE ORDER BY pp.priority
SELECT o.*, oi.* FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id WHERE o.user_id = ? ORDER BY o.created_at DESC LIMIT 50
SELECT f.* FROM fulfillment f WHERE f.order_id = ? AND f.status IN ('pending','allocated')

before_after_evidence.config_after: db.r6g.xlarge, 2 read replicas with R/W splitting, top 6 queries optimized
before_after_evidence.observed_outcome_summary: db_query_p95_latency_ms during business hours dropped from 450–820ms to under 220ms, application_p95_latency_ms dropped from peak 580–720ms back to 180–240ms, SLA 99.9% preserved.
before_after_evidence.source_attribution: Pattern observed across checkout-service cohort during Q4 2025.

content.headline: Severe SLA breach: P95 latency reaches 620-670ms during weekday business hours, more than 2× the 300ms SLA target, affecting 27.5% of all telemetry records for this tier-1 checkout service.
content.reasoning_summary: The application_p95_latency_ms distribution is strongly bimodal: 781 records cluster at 120-175ms (off-peak nights/weekends) but 370 records (27.5% of 1,344 total) breach the 300ms SLA, with values concentrated in the 563-674ms range during weekday business hours (9am–6pm, Mon–Fri). CPU_p95 correlates, rising from ~52% off-peak to ~67% during business hours on weekdays, suggesting the 8× m5.large fleet with target-tracking ASG (min=8, max=12) is insufficient for peak weekday load. Despite the auto-scaling policy, the fleet is not scaling out adequately to keep latency within SLA — the ASG max of 12 may be too low, or the scaling target/cooldown may be misconfigured. Given this is a tier-1 checkout service requiring 99.9% availability and P95 < 300ms, the sustained weekday breaches represent a critical optimization need: either increase ASG max/adjust scaling thresholds, or upsize instances to handle peak demand.

content.headline: Severe database query latency bottleneck: 6 slow queries drive p95 latency to 791ms during business hours, far exceeding the 300ms SLA target, with 365 SLA breaches across the observation window.
content.reasoning_summary: The database tier shows critical performance degradation during weekday business hours (09:00–18:00), with db_query_p95_latency_ms averaging 428–607ms by hour and peaking at 820ms around midday — 2.7× the 300ms SLA target. The top 6 queries (cart lookups at 820ms p95/6M executions, inventory joins at 680ms/3.5M, pricing lookups at 580ms/4M, order history at 510ms, payment providers at 440ms, fulfillment at 380ms) are all individually at or above the SLA threshold. The database is configured with only 1 read replica and no R/W splitting, meaning all read-heavy traffic hits the primary instance (db.r6g.xlarge). I/O wait p95 reaches 27ms, indicating disk pressure under load. No external cache tier exists to absorb repeated read patterns. Optimizing these 6 queries and adding read replicas with R/W splitting would address the root cause; the before/after evidence confirms this drops p95 latency to under 220ms.

content.reason: All 2 specialists completed; routing to Cross-Tier Evaluator for drift-check and synthesis.

cross_tier_interactions.interpretation: Database query P95 latency and application P95 latency move in near-perfect lockstep during weekday business hours with zero lag. The database time pattern (evidence_ref 53) shows db_query_p95_latency_ms surging from ~164ms at hour 8 to 428ms at hour 9, peaking at 608ms at hour 12; the compute time pattern (evidence_ref 42) shows application_p95_latency_ms surging from ~176ms at hour 8 to 422ms at hour 9, peaking at 514ms at hour 12. Both breach windows span the same weekday hours (09:00-18:00) with 365 database breaches (evidence_ref 55) and 370 compute breaches (evidence_ref 44). The zero-lag, near-identical temporal shape confirms the database is the upstream cause and compute latency is the downstream symptom — slow queries directly inflate application response times.

Database I/O wait and compute CPU P95 both rise during business hours. I/O wait P95 reaches 27ms (evidence_ref 32) while compute CPU P95 reaches 67-72% (evidence_ref 46). The compute CPU elevation is explained by request threads blocking on slow database I/O — threads hold compute resources longer when waiting for database responses, inflating apparent CPU utilization without compute being the actual bottleneck.

synthesis.root_cause: Slow database queries during weekday business hours (09:00–18:00) cascade into elevated application P95 latency on the compute tier, breaching the 300ms SLA target by more than 2× across 370 telemetry records.
synthesis.summary: Six unoptimized database queries with P95 latencies ranging from 380ms to 820ms on a single-replica db.r6g.xlarge with no R/W splitting cascade directly into application P95 latency of 620–670ms during weekday business hours, breaching the 300ms SLA in 370 of 1,344 records (27.5%). The six queries — cart lookups (820ms/6M executions), inventory joins (680ms/3.5M), pricing lookups (580ms/4M), order history (510ms/504K), payment providers (440ms/1.3M), and fulfillment (380ms/672K) — all lack covering indexes, and the single read replica without R/W splitting forces all read traffic onto the primary, compounding I/O wait (P95 27ms). Do NOT scale compute — the ASG fleet of 8× m5.large instances is correctly sized with CPU P95 at 67% and memory P95 at 64.4% during peak hours; the elevated application latency is reflected database latency, not a compute capacity deficit.
synthesis.headline: Database query bottleneck: 6 slow queries (P95 380–820ms) on single-replica db.r6g.xlarge cascade into application P95 of 620–670ms, breaching the 300ms SLA during all weekday business hours
synthesis.reasoning:
The cross-tier reconciliation establishes that the database tier is the upstream root cause and the compute tier is the downstream victim. Evidence:

1. **Temporal lockstep**: Database db_query_p95_latency_ms (evidence_ref=53) surges from ~164ms at hour 8 to 428ms at hour 9, peaking at 608ms at hour 12. Application P95 latency (evidence_ref=42) surges from ~176ms at hour 8 to 422ms at hour 9, peaking at 514ms at hour 12. Zero lag between the two signals, near-perfect correlation.

2. **Breach alignment**: 365 database breaches (evidence_ref=55) and 370 compute breaches (evidence_ref=44) occur in the same weekday business-hour windows (09:00–18:00).

3. **Compute is not saturated**: CPU P95 peaks at 67% (evidence_ref=46), memory P95 at 64.4% (evidence_ref=12). These are elevated but not at saturation levels that would independently produce 620–670ms latency spikes. The CPU elevation is explained by request threads holding compute resources while waiting on slow database I/O.

4. **Six slow queries identified**: (evidence_ref=34) all have P95 latencies at or above the 300ms SLA target. They lack covering indexes for their WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY patterns, causing full or partial table scans under load.

5. **Topology bottleneck**: The database (evidence_ref=51) runs on db.r6g.xlarge with only 1 read replica and no R/W splitting. All read-heavy checkout traffic hits the primary. I/O wait P95 of 27ms (evidence_ref=32) confirms disk pressure.

6. **Before/after validation**: Evidence_ref=22 confirms that optimizing the top 6 queries and adding 2 read replicas with R/W splitting drops application P95 from 580–720ms to 180–240ms, restoring SLA compliance.

The Compute Analyst's recommendation to scale compute is rejected: CPU and memory are not near saturation, and the temporal evidence proves the database is the upstream cause. Scaling compute would add cost without addressing the root cause.

telemetry_observations: db_query_p95_latency_ms = 791ms (P95), exceeding the 300ms SLA target by 2.6× (evidence_ref=26)
application_p95_latency_ms = 660ms (P95) during business hours, with 370 breaches of the 300ms SLA across the observation window (evidence_ref=14, evidence_ref=44)
db_io_wait_p95 = 27ms, indicating significant disk pressure on the primary instance (evidence_ref=32)
Compute CPU P95 = 67% during business hours, memory P95 = 64.4% — elevated but not saturated, confirming compute is not the bottleneck (evidence_ref=10, evidence_ref=12, evidence_ref=46)
6 slow queries with P95 latencies of 820ms, 680ms, 580ms, 510ms, 440ms, and 380ms across 16.1M total executions (evidence_ref=34)
infrastructure_context: Database: db.r6g.xlarge, 1 read replica, no R/W splitting, 500GB storage (evidence_ref=51)
Compute: 8× m5.large, target-tracking ASG min=8 max=12 (evidence_ref=16)
Current monthly cost: $6,400 total ($4,200 compute + $2,200 database) (evidence_ref=57)
correlation_observations: Database query P95 latency and application P95 latency move in near-perfect lockstep during weekday business hours with zero lag: db surges from 164ms to 608ms at hour 12, application surges from 176ms to 514ms at hour 12 (evidence_ref=42, evidence_ref=53)
365 database SLA breaches and 370 compute SLA breaches occur in identical weekday 09:00–18:00 windows (evidence_ref=44, evidence_ref=55)
Before/after evidence confirms optimizing 6 queries + adding 2 read replicas with R/W splitting drops application P95 from 580–720ms to 180–240ms (evidence_ref=22)

projected_state.notes: Application P95 latency drops from 620-670ms to 180-240ms during business hours, restoring SLA compliance (P95 < 300ms). Compute CPU P95 is expected to drop from 67% to 55-62% as request threads no longer block on slow database I/O. Memory remains stable around 60-65%.

cost_impact.notes: Adding 2 db.r6g.xlarge read replicas at $1,100/month each increases database tier cost from $2,200 to $4,400/month. Compute tier cost unchanged at $4,200/month. Total monthly cost increases from $6,400 to $8,600. This is a reliability investment to restore SLA compliance on a tier-1 checkout service, not a cost-reduction recommendation.

reconciliation.topology_assessment: Two specialists were invoked: the Compute Analyst and the Data Layer Analyst. No Network Analyst was invoked because the tier topology (evidence_ref=22) shows network as null — no network tier is present in the infrastructure. No cache tier exists either (cache=null in topology, confirmed by empty top_cache_keys in evidence_ref=36), but cache observations fall under the Data Layer Analyst's purview per docs/agents.md, and that specialist confirmed the absence of an external cache layer. The System Mapper's tier topology shows compute (8× m5.large with target-tracking ASG min=8/max=12) and database (db.r6g.xlarge, 1 replica, 500GB storage) as the only two active tiers. Both active tiers have dedicated specialist coverage.

specialist_findings_summary.key_observation: Application P95 latency is severely bimodal — 781 records cluster at 120-175ms (off-peak) while 370 records (27.5%) breach the 300ms SLA, concentrated at 563-674ms during weekday business hours (9am-6pm). CPU P95 rises from ~52% off-peak to ~67% during business hours on the 8× m5.large fleet, suggesting the compute tier is under pressure during peak periods, though CPU is not saturated.

Six slow queries drive db_query_p95_latency_ms to 791ms (p95) during business hours, with the worst query (cart lookups) at 820ms p95 across 6M executions. The database runs on a single db.r6g.xlarge with only 1 read replica and no R/W splitting, meaning all read-heavy traffic hits the primary. I/O wait p95 reaches 27ms indicating disk pressure. 365 SLA breaches were detected on the database tier, and before/after evidence confirms that optimizing the top 6 queries plus adding 2 read replicas with R/W splitting would drop application P95 latency to 180-240ms.

drift_check.narrative: The Compute Analyst correctly identified the SLA breach pattern (evidence_refs 38, 42, 44) and the bimodal latency distribution, but attributed causation to insufficient compute capacity (ASG max too low, scaling misconfigured). However, CPU P95 peaks at only ~67% during business hours (evidence_ref 46), and memory P95 is at 64.4% (evidence_ref 12) — neither metric is saturated. The Compute Analyst's own data shows CPU is elevated but not at a level that would independently produce 620-670ms latency spikes. The analyst noted the correlation between CPU rise and latency rise but did not consider that both could be symptoms of a downstream database bottleneck. The conclusion that compute scaling is needed is loosely supported: the compute tier is experiencing the symptom (high latency) but the root cause lies downstream.

The Data Layer Analyst's conclusion that slow database queries are the root cause is tightly supported by the evidence. The 6 top queries (evidence_ref 34) have individual P95 latencies ranging from 380ms to 820ms — every one at or above the 300ms SLA target. The time pattern (evidence_ref 53) shows db_query_p95_latency_ms averaging 428-607ms by hour during business hours, peaking at 820ms around midday, which directly explains the application-tier latency observed by the Compute Analyst. The database has only 1 replica with no R/W splitting (evidence_ref 51), and I/O wait P95 hits 27ms (evidence_ref 32), confirming disk pressure from unoptimized queries hitting the primary. The before/after evidence (evidence_ref 22) validates the proposed fix: optimizing the top 6 queries and adding 2 read replicas with R/W splitting drops application P95 to 180-240ms. The 365 database-tier breaches (evidence_ref 55) temporally align with the 370 compute-tier breaches (evidence_ref 44), reinforcing the cascade hypothesis.

cross_tier_correlations.interpretation: Database query P95 latency and application P95 latency move in near-perfect lockstep during weekday business hours with zero lag. The database time pattern (evidence_ref 53) shows db_query_p95_latency_ms surging from ~164ms at hour 8 to 428ms at hour 9, peaking at 608ms at hour 12; the compute time pattern (evidence_ref 42) shows application_p95_latency_ms surging from ~176ms at hour 8 to 422ms at hour 9, peaking at 514ms at hour 12. Both breach windows span the same weekday hours (09:00-18:00) with 365 database breaches (evidence_ref 55) and 370 compute breaches (evidence_ref 44). The zero-lag, near-identical temporal shape confirms the database is the upstream cause and compute latency is the downstream symptom — slow queries directly inflate application response times.

Database I/O wait and compute CPU P95 both rise during business hours. I/O wait P95 reaches 27ms (evidence_ref 32) while compute CPU P95 reaches 67-72% (evidence_ref 46). The compute CPU elevation is explained by request threads blocking on slow database I/O — threads hold compute resources longer when waiting for database responses, inflating apparent CPU utilization without compute being the actual bottleneck.

reconciliation.conflict_resolution: The two specialists disagree on root cause attribution. The Compute Analyst attributes the SLA breach to insufficient compute capacity (ASG max too low or scaling misconfigured). The Data Layer Analyst attributes it to slow database queries cascading into compute-tier latency. Resolution: the Data Layer Analyst's attribution is correct. The Compute Analyst's own evidence undermines the compute-scaling hypothesis — CPU P95 peaks at 67%, well below saturation, and memory P95 at 64.4% is healthy (evidence_refs 10, 12, 46). If compute were the bottleneck, CPU or memory would be near saturation during the breach windows. Instead, the temporal alignment between database query latency surges (evidence_refs 53, 55) and application latency surges (evidence_refs 42, 44) with zero lag demonstrates that the database tier is the upstream cause. The before/after evidence (evidence_ref 22) further confirms: optimizing the 6 queries and adding read replicas resolves the application latency — no compute change needed. The compute tier is correctly sized; it is experiencing reflected latency from database slowness, not a capacity deficit.

trade_off_analysis.note: Adding 2 db.r6g.xlarge read replicas at $1,100/month each raises database tier cost from $2,200 to $4,400/month. Total monthly spend increases from $6,400 to $8,600. No compute cost change — the 8× m5.large fleet at $4,200/month is retained as-is.

Covering indexes eliminate full/partial table scans on 6 queries totaling 16.1M executions. R/W splitting across 3 replicas distributes read-heavy checkout traffic off the primary, reducing I/O wait from 27ms P95. Combined effect validated by before/after evidence (evidence_ref=22): application P95 drops well below the 300ms SLA threshold. Compute CPU P95 expected to fall from 67% to 55–62% as request threads unblock.

370 of 1,344 telemetry records (27.5%) currently breach the 300ms SLA during weekday business hours. Post-change, application P95 of 180–240ms restores full SLA compliance. Additionally, moving from 1 to 3 read replicas improves database read-path redundancy — a single-replica failure no longer takes down all read traffic.

Index creation on large tables (carts: 6M rows, pricing: 4M rows) carries transient write amplification and lock risk during deployment; use CONCURRENTLY where supported. R/W splitting introduces replication-lag sensitivity for read-after-write patterns in the checkout flow — the application must tolerate or route those reads to the primary. Both risks are well-understood operational patterns with known mitigations. No risk from compute changes since none are proposed.

reflection.trade_off_philosophy:
This is a reliability investment, not a cost-reduction recommendation. The trade-off exchange is explicit: +$2,200/month buys SLA-compliant checkout flow by eliminating a 27.5% breach rate concentrated in every weekday business-hours window. The performance gain (−66% P95 latency) is the mechanism by which reliability is restored; cost is the price of the topology change (2 additional read replicas) that makes the performance gain durable under concurrent load.

Two alternatives were considered and rejected. First, the Compute Analyst's recommendation to scale compute (increase ASG max, upsize instances) was rejected because CPU P95 at 67% and memory P95 at 64.4% are not at saturation — the compute tier is experiencing reflected latency from the database, not a capacity deficit. Adding compute instances would increase cost without addressing the upstream root cause. Second, a phased approach (indexes first, replicas later) was rejected because the single-replica-no-R/W-splitting topology is a concurrent bottleneck independent of per-query optimization. Even with perfect indexes, funneling all read traffic through the primary during 16.1M daily query executions would leave residual contention. The before/after evidence validates the combined intervention, not either half in isolation. Both actions are needed in the same change window to restore SLA compliance.
reflection.evaluator_confidence_narrative:
Drift-check is tight on the Data Layer Analyst and loose on the Compute Analyst, with the conflict resolved decisively in favor of the database root cause. Three load-bearing observations license the recommendation:

(1) The 6 slow queries (evidence_ref=34) with individual P95 latencies of 380–820ms — every one at or above the 300ms SLA target — directly establish the per-query pathology. Without this observation, the recommendation would lack the specificity to name index columns.

(2) The zero-lag temporal lockstep between database query P95 (evidence_ref=53) and application P95 (evidence_ref=42) — surging in identical patterns from hour 8 through hour 17 with near-perfect correlation — is the load-bearing check that establishes the cascade direction (database → compute) and rules out the Compute Analyst's alternative hypothesis (compute capacity deficit). The complementary breach counts (365 database breaches in evidence_ref=55 vs. 370 compute breaches in evidence_ref=44 in the same windows) reinforce the cascade.

(3) The before/after evidence (evidence_ref=22) validates the combined intervention (indexes + 2 read replicas + R/W splitting) at the projected outcome level (180–240ms P95). Without this observation, the projected_state would be speculative rather than evidence-grounded.

The compute-is-healthy check (CPU P95 67% in evidence_ref=46, memory P95 64.4% in evidence_ref=12) is the load-bearing negative confirmation that rules out the compute-bottleneck alternative and justifies the "do NOT scale compute" directive. Cost figures are derived from evidence_ref=57 (current $6,400/month total, $2,200 database tier) and the known db.r6g.xlarge unit cost of $1,100/month, yielding a committed delta of +$2,200/month with no estimation uncertainty.

The six queries — cart lookups (820ms/6M executions), inventory joins (680ms/3.5M), pricing lookups (580ms/4M), order history (510ms/504K), payment providers (440ms/1.3M), and fulfillment (380ms/672K) — all lack covering indexes, and the single read replica without R/W splitting forces all read traffic onto the primary, compounding I/O wait (P95 27ms). Do NOT scale compute — the ASG fleet of 8× m5.large instances is correctly sized with CPU P95 at 67% and memory P95 at 64.4% during peak hours; the elevated application latency is reflected database latency, not a compute capacity deficit.

The System Mapper's tier topology shows compute (8× m5.large with target-tracking ASG min=8/max=12) and database (db.r6g.xlarge, 1 replica, 500GB storage) as the only two active tiers. Both active tiers have dedicated specialist coverage.

specialist_findings_summary.key_observation: Application P95 latency is severely bimodal — 781 records cluster at 120-175ms (off-peak) while 370 records (27.5%) breach the 300ms SLA, concentrated at 563-674ms during weekday business hours (9am-6pm). CPU P95 rises from ~52% off-peak to ~67% during business hours on the 8× m5.large fleet, suggesting the compute tier is under pressure during peak periods, though CPU is not saturated.

Six slow queries drive db_query_p95_latency_ms to 791ms (p95) during business hours, with the worst query (cart lookups) at 820ms p95 across 6M executions. The database runs on a single db.r6g.xlarge with only 1 read replica and no R/W splitting, meaning all read-heavy traffic hits the primary. I/O wait p95 reaches 27ms indicating disk pressure. 365 SLA breaches were detected on the database tier, and before/after evidence confirms that optimizing the top 6 queries plus adding 2 read replicas with R/W splitting would drop application P95 latency to 180-240ms.

drift_check.narrative: The Compute Analyst correctly identified the SLA breach pattern (evidence_refs 38, 42, 44) and the bimodal latency distribution, but attributed causation to insufficient compute capacity (ASG max too low, scaling misconfigured). However, CPU P95 peaks at only ~67% during business hours (evidence_ref 46), and memory P95 is at 64.4% (evidence_ref 12) — neither metric is saturated. The Compute Analyst's own data shows CPU is elevated but not at a level that would independently produce 620-670ms latency spikes. The analyst noted the correlation between CPU rise and latency rise but did not consider that both could be symptoms of a downstream database bottleneck. The conclusion that compute scaling is needed is loosely supported: the compute tier is experiencing the symptom (high latency) but the root cause lies downstream.

The Data Layer Analyst's conclusion that slow database queries are the root cause is tightly supported by the evidence. The 6 top queries (evidence_ref 34) have individual P95 latencies ranging from 380ms to 820ms — every one at or above the 300ms SLA target. The time pattern (evidence_ref 53) shows db_query_p95_latency_ms averaging 428-607ms by hour during business hours, peaking at 820ms around midday, which directly explains the application-tier latency observed by the Compute Analyst. The database has only 1 replica with no R/W splitting (evidence_ref 51), and I/O wait P95 hits 27ms (evidence_ref 32), confirming disk pressure from unoptimized queries hitting the primary. The before/after evidence (evidence_ref 22) validates the proposed fix: optimizing the top 6 queries and adding 2 read replicas with R/W splitting drops application P95 to 180-240ms. The 365 database-tier breaches (evidence_ref 55) temporally align with the 370 compute-tier breaches (evidence_ref 44), reinforcing the cascade hypothesis.

cross_tier_correlations.interpretation: Database query P95 latency and application P95 latency move in near-perfect lockstep during weekday business hours with zero lag. The database time pattern (evidence_ref 53) shows db_query_p95_latency_ms surging from ~164ms at hour 8 to 428ms at hour 9, peaking at 608ms at hour 12; the compute time pattern (evidence_ref 42) shows application_p95_latency_ms surging from ~176ms at hour 8 to 422ms at hour 9, peaking at 514ms at hour 12. Both breach windows span the same weekday hours (09:00-18:00) with 365 database breaches (evidence_ref 55) and 370 compute breaches (evidence_ref 44). The zero-lag, near-identical temporal shape confirms the database is the upstream cause and compute latency is the downstream symptom — slow queries directly inflate application response times.

Database I/O wait and compute CPU P95 both rise during business hours. I/O wait P95 reaches 27ms (evidence_ref 32) while compute CPU P95 reaches 67-72% (evidence_ref 46). The compute CPU elevation is explained by request threads blocking on slow database I/O — threads hold compute resources longer when waiting for database responses, inflating apparent CPU utilization without compute being the actual bottleneck.

reconciliation.conflict_resolution: The two specialists disagree on root cause attribution. The Compute Analyst attributes the SLA breach to insufficient compute capacity (ASG max too low or scaling misconfigured). The Data Layer Analyst attributes it to slow database queries cascading into compute-tier latency. Resolution: the Data Layer Analyst's attribution is correct. The Compute Analyst's own evidence undermines the compute-scaling hypothesis — CPU P95 peaks at 67%, well below saturation, and memory P95 at 64.4% is healthy (evidence_refs 10, 12, 46). If compute were the bottleneck, CPU or memory would be near saturation during the breach windows. Instead, the temporal alignment between database query latency surges (evidence_refs 53, 55) and application latency surges (evidence_refs 42, 44) with zero lag demonstrates that the database tier is the upstream cause. The before/after evidence (evidence_ref 22) further confirms: optimizing the 6 queries and adding read replicas resolves the application latency — no compute change needed. The compute tier is correctly sized; it is experiencing reflected latency from database slowness, not a capacity deficit.

trade_off_analysis.note: Adding 2 db.r6g.xlarge read replicas at $1,100/month each raises database tier cost from $2,200 to $4,400/month. Total monthly spend increases from $6,400 to $8,600. No compute cost change — the 8× m5.large fleet at $4,200/month is retained as-is.

Covering indexes eliminate full/partial table scans on 6 queries totaling 16.1M executions. R/W splitting across 3 replicas distributes read-heavy checkout traffic off the primary, reducing I/O wait from 27ms P95. Combined effect validated by before/after evidence (evidence_ref=22): application P95 drops well below the 300ms SLA threshold. Compute CPU P95 expected to fall from 67% to 55–62% as request threads unblock.

370 of 1,344 telemetry records (27.5%) currently breach the 300ms SLA during weekday business hours. Post-change, application P95 of 180–240ms restores full SLA compliance. Additionally, moving from 1 to 3 read replicas improves database read-path redundancy — a single-replica failure no longer takes down all read traffic.

Index creation on large tables (carts: 6M rows, pricing: 4M rows) carries transient write amplification and lock risk during deployment; use CONCURRENTLY where supported. R/W splitting introduces replication-lag sensitivity for read-after-write patterns in the checkout flow — the application must tolerate or route those reads to the primary. Both risks are well-understood operational patterns with known mitigations. No risk from compute changes since none are proposed.

reflection.trade_off_philosophy:
This is a reliability investment, not a cost-reduction recommendation. The trade-off exchange is explicit: +$2,200/month buys SLA-compliant checkout flow by eliminating a 27.5% breach rate concentrated in every weekday business-hours window. The performance gain (−66% P95 latency) is the mechanism by which reliability is restored; cost is the price of the topology change (2 additional read replicas) that makes the performance gain durable under concurrent load.

Two alternatives were considered and rejected. First, the Compute Analyst's recommendation to scale compute (increase ASG max, upsize instances) was rejected because CPU P95 at 67% and memory P95 at 64.4% are not at saturation — the compute tier is experiencing reflected latency from the database, not a capacity deficit. Adding compute instances would increase cost without addressing the upstream root cause. Second, a phased approach (indexes first, replicas later) was rejected because the single-replica-no-R/W-splitting topology is a concurrent bottleneck independent of per-query optimization. Even with perfect indexes, funneling all read traffic through the primary during 16.1M daily query executions would leave residual contention. The before/after evidence validates the combined intervention, not either half in isolation. Both actions are needed in the same change window to restore SLA compliance.
reflection.evaluator_confidence_narrative:
Drift-check is tight on the Data Layer Analyst and loose on the Compute Analyst, with the conflict resolved decisively in favor of the database root cause. Three load-bearing observations license the recommendation:

(1) The 6 slow queries (evidence_ref=34) with individual P95 latencies of 380–820ms — every one at or above the 300ms SLA target — directly establish the per-query pathology. Without this observation, the recommendation would lack the specificity to name index columns.

(2) The zero-lag temporal lockstep between database query P95 (evidence_ref=53) and application P95 (evidence_ref=42) — surging in identical patterns from hour 8 through hour 17 with near-perfect correlation — is the load-bearing check that establishes the cascade direction (database → compute) and rules out the Compute Analyst's alternative hypothesis (compute capacity deficit). The complementary breach counts (365 database breaches in evidence_ref=55 vs. 370 compute breaches in evidence_ref=44 in the same windows) reinforce the cascade.

(3) The before/after evidence (evidence_ref=22) validates the combined intervention (indexes + 2 read replicas + R/W splitting) at the projected outcome level (180–240ms P95). Without this observation, the projected_state would be speculative rather than evidence-grounded.

The compute-is-healthy check (CPU P95 67% in evidence_ref=46, memory P95 64.4% in evidence_ref=12) is the load-bearing negative confirmation that rules out the compute-bottleneck alternative and justifies the "do NOT scale compute" directive. Cost figures are derived from evidence_ref=57 (current $6,400/month total, $2,200 database tier) and the known db.r6g.xlarge unit cost of $1,100/month, yielding a committed delta of +$2,200/month with no estimation uncertainty.

content.reason: Evaluator produced a recommendation; routing to the Action Harness recommendation gate.
